findings.problem: The chapter keeps summarizing Doug's dead prepared act instead of making the reader sit through enough of the actual bad material. We are told he does 'borrowed comedy,' that the bits are merely 'present,' that the scripted opening is 'clean' and 'serviceable,' and that 'the reaching killed it,' but the embarrassing onstage content itself mostly stays off-page. In a comedy-ordeal chapter, that is sanitization by summary: the prose protects both writer and reader from the ugliest version of Doug's failure, which weakens the contrast when accidental truth finally works.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:7 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:9, replace some summary with 1-2 concrete borrowed lines or transitions that die in Cleveland. At snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:23 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:25, let the reader hear the actual replacement-cost turn or another scripted connective that loses Pittsburgh. At snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:71 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:119, render a short fragment of the prepared Detroit material before and after the live riff so the failure mode is audible, not just diagnosed.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if a cold reader can quote or paraphrase at least one actually bad prepared line or transition from Cleveland and one from Pittsburgh or Detroit, and can point to a concrete audience consequence attached to each by reading snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:7, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:23, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:71, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:119. Fail if those passages still rely mainly on labels such as 'borrowed,' 'serviceable,' or 'killed it' without showing what Doug said.

findings.problem: Doug's Detroit breakthrough is effective, but too many consecutive lines arrive as polished, detachable comic insights rather than speech produced under panic and discovery. 'Pauses with vending machines,' 'the parking lot from a second location,' 'nobody is there on purpose,' and 'nobody knows you're there' all land cleanly enough that the set starts sounding composed. That drifts from Doug's style-bible register, which is flat, literal, and only accidentally incisive, and it triggers the book's warning against literary polish and aphoristic dialogue.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the road/hotel material at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:93, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:101, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:105, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:109, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:113, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:117, but roughen the run so Doug sounds like he is discovering the bit in real time. Add at least one false start, self-correction, or over-specific dead-end detail, and roughen or cut the cleanest quotable lines so the monologue feels overheard rather than workshop-perfect.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, by reading the revised Detroit riff in snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:93 through snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:117, a cold reader can hear Doug thinking out loud rather than delivering six consecutive finished lines: at least one turn visibly self-corrects or wanders before landing, and no more than one sentence reads like a standalone aphorism without nearby concrete travel detail. Fail if the passage still reads as a chain of polished one-liners.

findings.problem: The chapter already makes its turn legible when Detroit stops laughing and starts listening, then goes polite again the moment Doug reaches for the set list. The exit call keeps re-proving the same point in fresher wording: 'Like they wanted the report, not the brochure' and 'The set list's a stack of forms.' Those lines are smart, but they explain a truth the scene has already shown, so the ending loses pressure by paraphrasing its own meaning instead of moving fully into the unsolved next problem.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:135, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:139, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:143, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:147, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:149, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:153, compress the phone call so it keeps the must-land beats but stops restating why Detroit worked. Preserve Doug's admission that the built material fails, Simone's 'I know,' and the two-weeks-to-taping pressure; then pivot the exchange toward the operational problem that the alive version still cannot be summoned on purpose.
findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised closing call still contains the admission that the material fails and the two-weeks deadline by reading snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:143 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:149, but adds one new operational consequence beyond the already-shown truth/listening dynamic and does not spend two separate metaphors paraphrasing the same lesson. Fail if the Detroit scene still lands its meaning at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:115 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:119 and the call afterward keeps explaining that meaning again.

summary: Fail. The chapter cleanly satisfies the required state shift, keeps continuity intact, and mostly holds Layla, Simone, and Doug's voices on-model, with no narrative-tense or canonical-fact contradictions detected. But it protects the ugliest parts of Doug's bombing by summarizing too much of the dead prepared material, lets the Detroit breakthrough sound more written than spoken, and then over-explains a turn the scene has already made legible, which keeps it below pass-level on award, craft, dialogue, and prose.
